• R = horizontal range
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity

The formula shows that the range is inversely proportional to gravitational acceleration. Therefore, if g decreases while u and θ remain constant, the horizontal range increases.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the quantities that remain unchanged.

The launch speed and projection angle are the same in both environments.

Step 2: Identify the quantity that changes.

Only the gravitational acceleration changes. The Moon has a much smaller gravitational acceleration than the Earth.

Step 3: Apply the range relationship.

Since R ∝ 1/g, a smaller value of g produces a larger horizontal range.

Step 4: Draw the conclusion.

The projectile stays in the air for a longer time on the Moon, allowing it to travel farther horizontally.

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because reaching a greater height does not reduce the range. The weaker gravity increases both the time of flight and the horizontal distance travelled.
Option C is incorrect because the range depends on gravitational acceleration as well as launch speed and projection angle.
Option D is incorrect because gravity on the Moon is weaker, not absent. Projectile motion still occurs.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever the launch speed and projection angle remain constant, remember that the horizontal range varies inversely with gravitational acceleration. Lower gravity means a longer time of flight and a greater range.
explanation.final_answer: A. The range is greater on the Moon because the weaker gravitational acceleration allows the projectile to remain in motion for a longer time.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine throwing the same ball in two places. On the Moon, gravity pulls the ball downward less strongly than on Earth. As a result, the ball stays in the air longer and travels farther before landing.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile to learn how launch speed, projection angle and gravitational acceleration affect horizontal range, with worked JAMB-style examples and beginner-friendly explanations in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question: Scientists in a university physics laboratory compare two identical projectile launchers operating on the same planet. Launcher P projects a ball with a speed of 28 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 35°, while Launcher Q projects another ball with a speed of 28 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 55°. Neglecting air resistance, which statement correctly compares the horizontal ranges of the two projectiles?

options.A: Launcher P produces a greater horizontal range than Launcher Q.
options.B: Launcher Q produces a greater horizontal range than Launcher P.
options.C: Both launchers produce the same horizontal range.
options.D: The horizontal range cannot be determined without knowing the mass of each ball.

answer_text: Both launchers produce the same horizontal range.

explanation.question_restate: This question compares the horizontal ranges of two projectiles launched with the same speed but at different angles. The objective is to determine whether one projectile travels farther horizontally than the other.
explanation.principle:
The horizontal range of a projectile is the total horizontal distance it travels before landing at the same level from which it was projected.

For a projectile launched and landing at the same height,

R = (u² sin 2θ) / g

where:

• R = horizontal range
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity.

An important property of this equation is that complementary angles produce the same range because they have the same value of sin 2θ.
explanation.definitions:
Complementary angles are two angles whose sum is 90°. Examples include 20° and 70°, 35° and 55°, and 40° and 50°.

When the launch speed and gravitational acceleration are unchanged, complementary angles always produce equal horizontal ranges.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the launch angles.

Launcher P is projected at 35° while Launcher Q is projected at 55°.

Step 2: Check whether the angles are complementary.

35° + 55° = 90°.

Therefore, they are complementary angles.

Step 3: Apply the range principle.

For Launcher P:

2θ = 70°

sin 70° ≈ 0.940.

For Launcher Q:

2θ = 110°

sin 110° = sin (180° − 110°) = sin 70° ≈ 0.940.

Step 4: Compare the remaining quantities.

Both projectiles have the same launch speed (28 m s⁻¹) and are launched under the same gravitational acceleration.

Since u² and g are identical and sin 2θ is also identical, both projectiles have exactly the same horizontal range.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

The correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because complementary angles do not produce different ranges when the launch speed is unchanged.
Option B is incorrect for the same reason. Neither launcher has a greater horizontal range.
Option D is incorrect because the mass of the projectile does not appear in the range equation. Under the stated assumptions, mass has no effect on the horizontal range.
common_misconceptions: Believing that a larger launch angle always produces a greater horizontal range.
Assuming complementary angles produce different ranges.
Thinking that the projectile's mass affects its horizontal range when air resistance is neglected.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever two launch angles add up to 90°, immediately check whether the launch speeds are the same. If they are, both projectiles will have the same horizontal range, provided they are launched and land at the same level.
explanation.final_answer: C. Both launchers produce the same horizontal range.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine kicking two footballs with the same force. One is kicked at 35° and the other at 55°. Although one travels higher while the other travels flatter, they land the same horizontal distance away because the angles are complementary.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for detailed lessons on the range formula, complementary angles, maximum range, worked JAMB-style examples, and exam strategies. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional guided practice.

question:
A football academy is evaluating four identical training machines. Each machine launches a football from ground level with the same initial speed of 28 m s⁻¹ but at different angles to the horizontal as shown below.

Machine P: 20°
Machine Q: 35°
Machine R: 45°
Machine S: 70°

Ignoring air resistance and taking g = 9.8 m s⁻², which machine will project the football through the greatest horizontal range?

explanation.question_restate: This question compares the horizontal ranges of projectiles launched with the same initial speed but at different angles. You are required to identify the launch angle that produces the greatest horizontal distance.
explanation.principle:
A projectile is an object that moves under the influence of gravity after being launched.

When a projectile is launched and lands at the same horizontal level, its horizontal range is given by

R = (u² sin 2θ) / g

where:

• R = horizontal range
• u = initial speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity.

Since all four machines use the same launch speed and operate under the same gravitational acceleration, only the value of sin 2θ determines which projectile travels the farthest.

The maximum value of sin 2θ is 1, which occurs when 2θ = 90°. Therefore, the greatest horizontal range is obtained when θ = 45°.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the factor that changes.

The launch speed and gravitational acceleration are the same for all four machines. Therefore, compare only the values of sin 2θ.

Step 2: Evaluate each launch angle.

Machine P: θ = 20° → sin 40° ≈ 0.643

Machine Q: θ = 35° → sin 70° ≈ 0.940

Machine R: θ = 45° → sin 90° = 1.000

Machine S: θ = 70° → sin 140° = sin 40° ≈ 0.643

Step 3: Compare the values.

The largest value is 1.000 for Machine R.

Therefore, Machine R gives the greatest horizontal range.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because a launch angle of 20° does not produce the maximum value of sin 2θ. Its range is much smaller than that at 45°.
Option B is incorrect because although 35° gives a large range, sin 70° is still less than sin 90°, so it does not produce the maximum range.
Option D is incorrect because many candidates wrongly assume a larger launch angle always gives a larger range. At 70°, the range is the same as that for 20°, not the greatest.
explanation.exam_tip:
For projectiles launched and landing at the same level, always remember:

• Maximum horizontal range occurs at 45°.
• Complementary angles such as 20° and 70° produce the same horizontal range because they have the same value of sin 2θ.
• Do not assume that the largest launch angle gives the greatest range.
explanation.simple_explanation:
Imagine kicking four footballs with exactly the same force but at different angles.

The ball kicked at 45° travels the farthest horizontally. Kicking much lower or much higher reduces the horizontal distance. Therefore, the football launched by Machine R covers the greatest range.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile to learn how launch angle, launch speed, complementary angles and gravity affect horizontal range. Continue practising with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional JAMB-standard worked examples.

question:
During a WAEC practical, a teacher asked students to verify how the horizontal range of a projectile depends on the angle of projection. A spring launcher was adjusted to fire identical steel balls with the same launch speed at four different angles, and the average horizontal ranges obtained are shown below.

| Angle (°) | Average Range (m) |
|-----------|-------------------|
| 20        | 26.5              |
| 35        | 40.1              |
| 45        | 43.2              |
| 70        | 26.7              |

Which conclusion is best supported by the experimental results?

options.A: The horizontal range increases continuously as the angle of projection increases.
options.B: The greatest horizontal range is obtained when the angle of projection is approximately 45°.
options.C: The horizontal range depends only on the launch speed and is independent of the angle of projection.
options.D: The maximum horizontal range occurs when the projectile is launched vertically.

answer_text: The greatest horizontal range is obtained when the angle of projection is approximately 45°.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to interpret experimental data on projectile range rather than substitute values into a formula. You must identify the conclusion that agrees with the observations.
explanation.principle:
The horizontal range of a projectile projected and landing at the same horizontal level is given by

R = (u² sin 2θ) / g

where:
• R is the horizontal range.
• u is the launch speed.
• θ is the angle of projection.
• g is the acceleration due to gravity.

When the launch speed remains constant, the range depends on the value of sin 2θ. The largest possible value of sin 2θ is 1, which occurs when 2θ = 90°, giving θ = 45°. Therefore, a launch angle of about 45° produces the maximum horizontal range.
steps: Step 1: Examine the experimental table carefully. The measured ranges are 26.5 m, 40.1 m, 43.2 m and 26.7 m.
Step 2: Identify the largest measured value. The greatest range is 43.2 m, which corresponds to a launch angle of 45°.
Step 3: Compare this observation with each option.
Step 4: Option B agrees exactly with the experimental evidence, while the other options contradict either the measurements or the theory of projectile motion.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the range increases from 20° to 45° but decreases again at 70°.
Option C is incorrect because changing only the angle while keeping the launch speed constant clearly changes the measured range.
Option D is incorrect because a vertically projected object has no horizontal motion and therefore has zero horizontal range.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB or WAEC presents experimental data, base your answer on the evidence in the table before recalling theory. Also remember that for projectiles landing at the same level, the maximum horizontal range occurs at about 45°, while complementary angles produce approximately equal ranges.
explanation.final_answer: B. The greatest horizontal range is obtained when the angle of projection is approximately 45°.
explanation.simple_explanation: The experiment shows that the projectile travelled the farthest at 45°. At smaller or larger angles, the horizontal distance became shorter. This confirms the well-known result that a projectile has its maximum range when launched at about 45° on level ground.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for lessons on the range equation, complementary angles, maximum range, experimental verification and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice.

question:
The results of a projectile-range experiment carried out in a motion analysis laboratory are shown below.

| Trial | Launch speed (m s⁻¹) | Angle of projection | Measured range (m) |
|-------|-----------------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| I     | 26                    | 35°                 | 63                 |
| II    | 26                    | 55°                 | 63                 |
| III   | 26                    | 45°                 | 69                 |
| IV    | 26                    | 70°                 | 44                 |

Ignoring air resistance, which conclusion is best supported by the experimental data?

options.A: The greatest horizontal range is obtained when the projectile is projected at 45°.
options.B: The horizontal range increases continuously as the angle of projection increases.
options.C: Projectiles launched at complementary angles always have different horizontal ranges.
options.D: The horizontal range depends only on the launch speed and is independent of the angle of projection.

answer_text: The greatest horizontal range is obtained when the projectile is projected at 45°.

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to interpret experimental data obtained from a projectile-range investigation and identify the conclusion that agrees with the observed results.
explanation.principle:
The horizontal range of a projectile is the horizontal distance travelled before it returns to the same level.

For a projectile projected and landing at the same level,

R = (u² sin 2θ) / g

where:
• R = horizontal range
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity.

For a fixed launch speed, the range depends on the value of sin 2θ. The maximum value of sin 2θ is 1, which occurs when 2θ = 90°, giving θ = 45°. Also, complementary angles such as 35° and 55° produce equal ranges because they have the same value of sin 2θ.
steps: Step 1: Observe that all four trials use the same launch speed of 26 m s⁻¹. Therefore, any change in range is due to the angle of projection.

Step 2: Compare the measured ranges.

35° → 63 m
55° → 63 m
45° → 69 m
70° → 44 m
Step 3: Notice that the largest measured range is 69 m, corresponding to a launch angle of 45°.
Step 4: Also observe that 35° and 55° are complementary angles and produce the same measured range, which agrees with projectile theory.
Step 5: Therefore, the experimental data support the conclusion that the maximum horizontal range is obtained at a projection angle of 45°.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because the range does not increase continuously with angle. After 45°, it decreases, as shown by the 70° trial.
Option C is incorrect because the data show that 35° and 55°, which are complementary angles, produce the same measured range.
Option D is incorrect because although the launch speed is constant in every trial, the measured ranges are different. This shows that the angle of projection also affects the range.
explanation.exam_tip:
When interpreting projectile-range tables in JAMB, first check whether the launch speed is constant. If it is, compare only the effect of the projection angle. Remember these facts:

• Maximum range occurs at 45°.
• Complementary angles produce equal ranges.
• Increasing the angle beyond 45° reduces the horizontal range.
explanation.final_answer: A. The greatest horizontal range is obtained when the projectile is projected at 45°.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine kicking four footballs with the same force but at different angles. The kick at 45° travels the farthest, while kicks at complementary angles such as 35° and 55° travel the same horizontal distance. This is exactly what the experimental results show.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for complete lessons on the range equation, complementary angles, maximum range, experimental interpretation, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your revision with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice and exam-focused guidance.

question: While reviewing solutions during a physics tutorial, an instructor noticed that a candidate calculated the horizontal range of a projectile launched at 45° with a speed of 28 m s⁻¹ using the expression R = (u² sin θ)/g instead of the correct range formula. Taking g = 9.8 m s⁻², which statement best explains the candidate's error?

options.A: The candidate should have used the maximum height formula instead of the range formula.
options.B: The candidate used sin θ instead of sin 2θ, thereby applying the wrong projectile range formula.
options.C: The candidate should have used cos 2θ because horizontal motion determines the range.
options.D: The candidate should have multiplied the launch speed by the time of flight without considering the launch angle.

answer_text: The candidate used sin θ instead of sin 2θ, thereby applying the wrong projectile range formula.

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your ability to identify an error in the application of the projectile range formula rather than perform a lengthy calculation.
explanation.principle:
For a projectile that is projected and lands at the same horizontal level, the horizontal range is given by:

R = (u² sin 2θ)/g

where:
• R = horizontal range
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity.

The angle appears as 2θ because the range equation is obtained by combining the horizontal and vertical components of motion. Replacing sin 2θ with sin θ changes the physics of the problem and gives an incorrect result.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the correct formula.

The correct horizontal range formula is R = (u² sin 2θ)/g.

Step 2: Compare it with the candidate's formula.

The candidate used R = (u² sin θ)/g.

The factor sin 2θ has been incorrectly replaced by sin θ.

Step 3: Verify using the given angle.

For θ = 45°,

sin 2θ = sin 90° = 1,

whereas

sin 45° ≈ 0.707.

Using sin 45° would underestimate the range.

Step 4: Select the statement that correctly identifies the mistake.

Only Option B correctly explains that the candidate used the wrong trigonometric term in the range formula.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the maximum height formula is used to determine the greatest vertical height reached, not the horizontal range.
Option C is incorrect because the range equation does not use cos 2θ.

Option D is incorrect because horizontal range equals horizontal velocity multiplied by time of flight. The launch angle is already accounted for through the horizontal velocity component and the time of flight, so it cannot be ignored.
explanation.exam_tip:
When solving projectile questions, first identify the quantity being asked.

For horizontal range at the same level, always remember:

R = (u² sin 2θ)/g

A common JAMB trap is replacing sin 2θ with sin θ or confusing the range formula with those for maximum height or time of flight.
explanation.final_answer: B. The candidate used sin θ instead of sin 2θ, thereby applying the wrong projectile range formula.
explanation.simple_explanation: Think of the range formula as a complete recipe. Leaving out the factor 2 in the angle is like using the wrong ingredient. Even if all the other values are correct, the final answer will still be wrong because the correct formula requires sin 2θ, not sin θ.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for a detailed explanation of the derivation of the range formula, common examination mistakes, worked examples, complementary angles, and JAMB-focused practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for more guided lessons and revision exercises.

question: A basketball coach is helping players improve the distance of their long overhead passes. During a training session, the coach uses a launching machine to project identical basketballs from the same height with the same initial speed of 28 m s⁻¹ but at different angles. Ignoring air resistance and taking g = 9.8 m s⁻², which launch angle will produce the greatest horizontal range?

explanation.question_restate: This question assesses your understanding of the launch angle that produces the maximum horizontal range when a projectile is launched and lands at the same horizontal level.
explanation.principle:
The horizontal range of a projectile is the total horizontal distance it travels before landing.

For a projectile launched and landing at the same level,

R = (u² sin 2θ) / g

where:
• R = horizontal range
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity

For a fixed launch speed and constant gravitational acceleration, the range depends entirely on the value of sin 2θ.
explanation.definitions:
Projectile range is the horizontal distance travelled by a projectile before it returns to its original level.

The launch angle is the angle the projectile makes with the horizontal at the instant of projection.
explanation.foundational_knowledge:
The value of sin 2θ varies with the launch angle.

Its maximum possible value is 1, which occurs when:

2θ = 90°

Therefore,

θ = 45°

Hence, a projectile launched at 45° has the greatest possible horizontal range for a given speed when it lands at the same level.
explanation.formula_introduction:
Since

R = (u² sin 2θ) / g,

and u and g remain constant, compare only sin 2θ:

• 30° → sin 60° = 0.866
• 45° → sin 90° = 1.000
• 60° → sin 120° = 0.866
• 75° → sin 150° = 0.500
steps:
Step 1: Identify that the launch speed is the same for all four cases.

Therefore, the range depends only on sin 2θ.

Step 2: Evaluate sin 2θ for each angle.

30° → sin 60° = 0.866
45° → sin 90° = 1.000
60° → sin 120° = 0.866
75° → sin 150° = 0.500

Step 3: Compare the values.

The largest value is 1.000, corresponding to a launch angle of 45°.

Step 4: Conclude that the projectile launched at 45° will travel the greatest horizontal distance.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because although 30° gives a long range, it does not produce the maximum. Its range equals that of its complementary angle, 60°.
Option C is incorrect because 60° is complementary to 30°. Both produce the same range, which is less than the maximum at 45°.
Option D is incorrect because a larger launch angle does not necessarily produce a larger range. At 75°, much of the initial speed is directed vertically, reducing the horizontal distance travelled.
common_misconceptions: Believing that the largest launch angle always produces the greatest range.
Confusing maximum height with maximum horizontal range.
Forgetting that complementary angles produce equal ranges when the launch speed is unchanged.
Assuming the launch speed must be substituted into the formula even when only a comparison of ranges is required.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB asks which launch angle gives the greatest horizontal range for a projectile that lands at the same level, immediately remember that the answer is 45°. Also remember that complementary angles such as 30° and 60° produce equal ranges.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine throwing two identical balls with the same force. Throwing one at 45° gives the best balance between moving upward and moving forward. This balance allows the ball to stay in the air long enough while still travelling far horizontally, producing the greatest range.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for a complete lesson on projectile range, complementary angles, maximum range, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional exam-focused practice.

question: Agricultural engineers are calibrating a fertiliser spreader that throws fertiliser pellets from ground level. To ensure uniform distribution, the pellets are projected at a speed of 25 m s⁻¹ and an angle of 37° to the horizontal. Taking g = 10 m s⁻² and sin74° = 0.96, what horizontal distance will a pellet travel before landing on the ground?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the horizontal range of a fertiliser pellet projected from ground level using the projectile range equation.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known quantities.

Launch speed, u = 25 m s⁻¹

Angle of projection, θ = 37°

Acceleration due to gravity, g = 10 m s⁻²

sin74° = 0.96

Step 2: Apply the range formula.

R = (u² sin2θ)/g

Step 4: Compare the result with the options.

The calculated horizontal range is 60 m.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (48 m) may result from using an incorrect trigonometric value or making an arithmetic error during substitution.
Option C (72 m) may result from multiplying incorrectly after substitution or using an incorrect value of sin2θ.
Option D (96 m) may result from confusing the range formula with another projectile equation or omitting division by g.
explanation.exam_tip: For projectiles that land at the same level from which they are launched, always use R = (u² sin2θ)/g. One of the most common JAMB mistakes is using sinθ instead of sin2θ or forgetting to square the launch speed.
explanation.simple_explanation: The fertiliser pellet moves forward while gravity pulls it downward. The horizontal distance it covers before landing is called its range. Using the projectile range formula gives a distance of 60 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for a complete lesson on projectile range, complementary angles, maximum range, reverse calculations, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional exam-focused practice.

question: Engineers in a manufacturing plant are calibrating an automated launcher used to transfer lightweight components across a production line. The launcher projects each component at an angle of 45° to the horizontal with a speed of 28 m s⁻¹. Assuming the component lands at the same horizontal level from which it was launched and taking g = 9.8 m s⁻², what horizontal range will the component travel?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the horizontal range of a projectile launched from an industrial automated system. Since the projectile is launched and lands at the same level, the standard projectile-range equation applies.
explanation.principle:
The horizontal range of a projectile is the total horizontal distance it travels before returning to its original level.

For projectiles launched and landing at the same height,

R = (u² sin 2θ) / g

where:

• R = horizontal range (m)
• u = launch speed (m s⁻¹)
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity (m s⁻²)

When θ = 45°, then 2θ = 90° and sin 90° = 1. This gives the maximum possible range for a given launch speed.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

u = 28 m s⁻¹
θ = 45°
g = 9.8 m s⁻²

Step 2: Write the range formula.

R = (u² sin 2θ) / g

Step 3: Evaluate sin 2θ.

2 × 45° = 90°

sin 90° = 1

Step 4: Substitute the values.

R = (28² × 1) / 9.8

= 784 / 9.8

= 80 m

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

The calculated horizontal range is 80 m.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (40 m) is too small and may result from incorrectly halving the calculated range.
Option B (60 m) could arise from an arithmetic error or incorrect substitution into the formula.
Option D (100 m) is larger than the correct value and may result from using an incorrect value of gravitational acceleration or calculation error.
explanation.exam_tip:
For projectiles that are launched and land at the same height, always check whether the angle is 45°. If it is, then sin 2θ = sin 90° = 1, making the calculation much simpler.

Remember that maximum horizontal range occurs at a launch angle of 45°.
explanation.simple_explanation: The launcher throws the component at 45°, which gives the greatest possible horizontal distance for that speed. Using the projectile-range formula gives a horizontal distance of 80 m before the component lands.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for a complete lesson on projectile range, complementary angles, maximum range, worked examples, reverse calculations, and JAMB-focused practice available in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question: Engineers testing an automated pipeline inspection system launch a sensor capsule from ground level at a speed of 40 m s⁻¹ and an angle of 30° above the horizontal. Assuming the capsule lands at the same horizontal level and neglecting air resistance, what horizontal distance does it travel before landing? (Take g = 10 m s⁻² and sin60° = 0.866.)

explanation.question_restate: This question applies the projectile range equation to determine how far a sensor capsule travels horizontally during an oil and gas pipeline inspection test.
explanation.principle:
When a projectile is launched and lands at the same horizontal level, its horizontal range is the total horizontal distance travelled before landing.

The range depends on three quantities:

• launch speed (u)
• angle of projection (θ)
• acceleration due to gravity (g)

The appropriate formula is:

R = (u² sin2θ) / g

where:

• R = horizontal range
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity

The factor sin2θ accounts for both the horizontal and vertical components of the projectile's motion.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

u = 40 m s⁻¹
θ = 30°
g = 10 m s⁻²
sin60° = 0.866

Step 2: Write the projectile range formula.

R = (u² sin2θ) / g

Step 3: Substitute the given values.

R = (40² × 0.866) / 10

Step 5: Compare with the options.

The calculated horizontal range is 138.6 m.

Therefore, Option A is correct.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it is approximately half the correct range, a common error resulting from omitting the factor sin2θ or dividing twice.
Option C is incorrect because it is obtained by ignoring the trigonometric factor and calculating u²/g only.
Option D is incorrect because it results from using an incorrect projectile formula or confusing range with another quantity.
explanation.exam_tip:
For projectiles that are launched and land at the same level, always use R = (u² sin2θ) / g.

Remember that the angle in the sine function is doubled. Candidates often substitute sinθ instead of sin2θ, leading to incorrect answers.
explanation.simple_explanation: The sensor capsule behaves like any projectile. Since it starts and finishes at the same height, the horizontal distance it travels is found using the range formula. Substituting the given values gives approximately 138.6 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for detailed lessons on calculating projectile range, complementary angles, maximum range, reverse calculations, and JAMB-style worked examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional practice and exam-focused revision.

question:
An emergency response agency is testing a drone that releases a medical package to victims in an isolated area. During one test, the package is launched from ground level at a speed of 35 m s⁻¹ and lands back at the same horizontal level after travelling 105 m.

Assume g = 10 m s⁻².

Which angle of projection was most likely used?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the launch angle of a projectile from its horizontal range and launch speed. It is a reverse projectile problem because the range is known while the angle is unknown.
explanation.principle:
The horizontal range of a projectile is the total horizontal distance travelled before it returns to the same level from which it was projected.

For projection and landing at the same horizontal level, the range is given by:

R = (u² sin2θ)/g

where:

• R = horizontal range
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity

In reverse problems, we substitute the known values into the formula and determine the value of sin2θ before identifying the corresponding angle.
steps:
Step 1: List the known quantities.

Launch speed, u = 35 m s⁻¹
Range, R = 105 m
Acceleration due to gravity, g = 10 m s⁻²

Step 2: Write the range formula.

R = (u² sin2θ)/g

Step 3: Substitute the known values.

105 = (35² × sin2θ)/10

105 = (1225 × sin2θ)/10

Step 4: Solve for sin2θ.

1050 = 1225 sin2θ

sin2θ = 1050/1225 = 0.857

Since sin60° ≈ 0.866, the nearest standard value is:

2θ ≈ 60°

Step 5: Determine the launch angle.

θ = 60°/2 = 30°

Therefore, the required angle of projection is 30°.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because a launch angle of 45° would give the maximum range for the same launch speed, which is greater than 105 m.

Option C is incorrect because although 60° is complementary to 30° and produces the same range, it cannot be the correct answer here since substituting θ = 60° gives 2θ = 120°, not the calculated principal value of approximately 60° used in the reverse solution.
Option D is incorrect because a launch angle of 75° would produce a much smaller horizontal range than the value given.
explanation.exam_tip: For reverse projectile questions, first calculate sin2θ. Then identify the corresponding value of 2θ before dividing by two to obtain the launch angle. Remember that JAMB often expects the principal acute angle unless the question specifically asks for both complementary angles.
explanation.simple_explanation: The package travelled 105 m after being launched at 35 m s⁻¹. Using the projectile range formula gives sin2θ ≈ 0.857, which is closest to sin60°. Therefore, 2θ ≈ 60°, so θ ≈ 30°. Hence, the correct answer is Option A.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for a complete lesson on calculating projectile range, determining launch angles, complementary angles, maximum range, and solving reverse JAMB-style projectile problems. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional worked examples and exam-focused practice.

question:
An environmental agency is testing a flood-monitoring drone that releases a sensor package onto a designated landing point. During one test, the package is projected from ground level with a speed of 35 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 30° to the horizontal. Neglecting air resistance and take g = 10 m s⁻².

Approximately how far from the launch point will the sensor package land?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the horizontal range of a projectile released by a flood-monitoring drone using the standard projectile range equation.
explanation.principle:
A projectile is an object launched into the air and allowed to move under the influence of gravity alone, provided air resistance is neglected.

The horizontal range is the total horizontal distance travelled before the projectile returns to the same horizontal level.

For projection and landing at the same level,

R = (u² sin 2θ)/g

where:
• R = horizontal range (m)
• u = launch speed (m s⁻¹)
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity (m s⁻²)

The factor sin 2θ accounts for the effect of the launch angle on the horizontal distance travelled.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known quantities.

u = 35 m s⁻¹
θ = 30°
g = 10 m s⁻²

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

R = (u² sin 2θ)/g

Step 3: Evaluate the trigonometric term.

2θ = 60°

sin 60° = 0.866

Step 5: Compare the result with the options.

The calculated horizontal range is approximately 106.1 m.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A (53.0 m) is approximately half of the correct value and may result from an incorrect substitution or arithmetic error.
Option B (92.4 m) could result from using an incorrect trigonometric value or an inappropriate projectile formula.
Option D (122.5 m) is obtained by ignoring the effect of the launch angle and incorrectly assuming the maximum possible range for the given speed.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a projectile is launched and lands at the same horizontal level, first check whether the range formula applies directly. Remember to evaluate sin 2θ, not sin θ. This is one of the most common mistakes made in JAMB projectile questions.
explanation.simple_explanation: The sensor package travels forward while gravity pulls it downward. The range formula combines the launch speed, projection angle and gravity to determine how far it travels horizontally before landing. Substituting the given values gives a range of about 106.1 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for detailed lessons on projectile range, complementary angles, maximum range, worked examples and JAMB-focused practice available in the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question: A robotics laboratory is testing an autonomous launcher that projects inspection capsules onto a designated landing zone. During one test, the launcher fires a capsule at a speed of 35 m s⁻¹ and an angle of 30° above the horizontal. Assuming the capsule lands at the same horizontal level from which it was launched and take g = 10 m s⁻², what horizontal range will the capsule travel?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the horizontal range of a projectile launched from and landing on the same horizontal level using the standard projectile-range equation.
explanation.principle:
The horizontal range of a projectile is the total horizontal distance travelled before it lands.

When the projectile is launched and lands at the same level, its range is given by:

R = (u² sin 2θ) / g

where:

• R = horizontal range
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity

The factor sin 2θ shows that both the launch speed and launch angle determine how far the projectile travels horizontally.
steps:
Step 1: Write down the known values.

u = 35 m s⁻¹
θ = 30°
g = 10 m s⁻²

Step 2: Determine twice the launch angle.

2θ = 60°

sin 60° = 0.866

Step 3: Substitute into the range formula.

R = (35² × 0.866) / 10

= (1225 × 0.866) / 10

= 1060.85 / 10

= 106.085 m

Step 4: Round appropriately.

R ≈ 106.1 m

Step 5: Compare the calculated value with the options.

The correct answer is Option D.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is too small and may result from using an incorrect trigonometric value or omitting the factor sin 2θ.
Option B is incorrect because it arises from an incomplete application of the projectile-range formula.
Option C is incorrect because it underestimates the effect of the launch speed and does not agree with the correct calculation.
Option D is correct because it is obtained by correctly applying R = (u² sin 2θ) / g.
explanation.exam_tip: For projectiles that land at the same level, always remember to use sin 2θ, not sin θ or cos θ. Also note that complementary angles produce the same range when the launch speed is unchanged.
explanation.simple_explanation: The launcher throws the capsule with a speed of 35 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 30°. Using the projectile-range formula gives a horizontal distance of about 106.1 m before the capsule lands.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for detailed lessons on calculating projectile range, complementary angles, maximum range, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional exam-focused practice.

question: Engineers testing a coastal surveillance launcher project a tracking beacon from ground level at a speed of 40 m s⁻¹ and an angle of 30° to the horizontal. Assuming the beacon lands at the same horizontal level from which it was launched and taking g = 10 m s⁻², what horizontal range will the beacon cover?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to determine the horizontal range of a projectile launched from and landing on the same horizontal level using the projectile range equation.
explanation.principle:
The horizontal range of a projectile is the total horizontal distance travelled before it lands.

For a projectile launched and landing at the same level,

R = (u² sin2θ)/g

where:
• R = horizontal range (m)
• u = launch speed (m s⁻¹)
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity (m s⁻²)

The term sin2θ accounts for the combined effect of the horizontal and vertical components of the launch velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the known values.

u = 40 m s⁻¹
θ = 30°
g = 10 m s⁻²

Step 2: Write the appropriate formula.

R = (u² sin2θ)/g

Step 3: Evaluate sin2θ.

2θ = 60°

sin60° = 0.866

Step 5: Round to one decimal place.

R ≈ 138.6 m

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because it is approximately half the correct range and could result from an incorrect substitution or arithmetic error.
Option C is incorrect because it assumes sin2θ = 1, which is true only for a launch angle of 45°.
Option D is incorrect because it may result from using the horizontal component of velocity directly instead of the projectile range formula.
common_misconceptions: Using sinθ instead of sin2θ in the range equation.
Assuming the maximum range always occurs regardless of the launch angle.
Substituting the horizontal component of velocity directly into the formula.
Forgetting to square the launch speed before substitution.
explanation.exam_tip: For projectiles that are launched and land at the same horizontal level, always begin with R = (u² sin2θ)/g. Remember that the angle in the sine function is doubled before evaluating the trigonometric value.
explanation.simple_explanation: The launcher throws the beacon at an angle, so both its horizontal and vertical motions determine how far it travels. Applying the projectile range formula gives a horizontal distance of approximately 138.6 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile to learn how launch speed, projection angle, complementary angles and gravity affect projectile range, with fully worked JAMB-style examples and practice questions.

question: At an aerospace testing facility, engineers are evaluating a prototype launcher designed to release emergency payloads over a fixed horizontal distance. During one test, the launcher projects a payload from ground level with a speed of 40 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 30° to the horizontal. Assuming the payload lands at the same horizontal level and take g = 10 m s⁻², which recommendation would correctly improve the horizontal range without changing the launch speed?

explanation.question_restate: This question evaluates your understanding of how the angle of projection affects the horizontal range of a projectile when the launch speed remains constant.
explanation.principle:
For a projectile that is launched and lands at the same horizontal level, the horizontal range is given by

R = (u² sin2θ)/g

where:

• R is the horizontal range.
• u is the launch speed.
• θ is the angle of projection.
• g is the acceleration due to gravity.

Since the launch speed and gravitational acceleration remain unchanged, the only factor affecting the range is the value of sin2θ. The maximum possible value of sin2θ is 1, which occurs when 2θ = 90°, giving θ = 45°. Therefore, a launch angle of 45° produces the maximum horizontal range.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the current launch angle.

The launcher is currently set at 30°.

Step 2: Compare the proposed launch angles.

For each option, evaluate sin2θ:

• 45° → sin90° = 1.000
• 75° → sin150° = 0.500
• 15° → sin30° = 0.500
• 90° → sin180° = 0

Step 3: Determine which angle gives the greatest range.

Since sin90° is the largest possible value, a launch angle of 45° produces the maximum horizontal range.

Step 4: Select the correct recommendation.

The engineers should adjust the launcher to 45°.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because although 75° gives a longer time of flight, its horizontal range is smaller than that at 45°.
Option C is incorrect because 15° and 75° produce the same range as complementary angles, and both are less than the maximum range.
Option D is incorrect because at 90° the projectile travels vertically upward and returns to the launch point, giving zero horizontal range.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever a JAMB question asks for the maximum horizontal range of a projectile projected and landing at the same level, immediately recall that the optimum launch angle is 45°. Do not confuse maximum range with maximum height, which increases as the launch angle approaches 90°.
explanation.final_answer: A. Increase the launch angle to 45°.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine throwing a ball. Throwing it straight up (90°) makes it go very high but not far. Throwing it too low also reduces the distance. A launch angle of 45° provides the best balance between upward and forward motion, allowing the ball to travel the greatest horizontal distance.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for detailed lessons on the range equation, complementary angles, maximum range, worked examples, and JAMB-style practice questions. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional exam-focused revision.

question:
An autonomous logistics company is evaluating four launch configurations for a ground-based package delivery system. In each test, a package is projected from and lands on the same horizontal level. Air resistance is neglected and the acceleration due to gravity is 10 m s⁻².

The launch conditions are:

• Test I: Speed = 28 m s⁻¹, Angle = 45°
• Test II: Speed = 32 m s⁻¹, Angle = 30°
• Test III: Speed = 36 m s⁻¹, Angle = 25°
• Test IV: Speed = 24 m s⁻¹, Angle = 60°

Using the projectile range equation, which launch configuration will produce the greatest horizontal range?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to compare the horizontal ranges of four projectiles launched under different speeds and angles to determine which one travels the greatest horizontal distance.
explanation.principle:
The horizontal range of a projectile is the total horizontal distance travelled before it returns to the same horizontal level.

For a projectile launched and landing at the same level,

R = (u² sin 2θ) / g

where:

• R = horizontal range
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity.

The range depends on both the square of the launch speed and the value of sin 2θ. Therefore, the projectile with the highest speed does not necessarily have the greatest range unless its launch angle is also favourable.
steps:
Step 1: Calculate the range for Test I.

R = (28² × sin 90°) / 10
= (784 × 1) / 10
= 78.4 m.

Step 2: Calculate the range for Test II.

R = (32² × sin 60°) / 10
= (1024 × 0.866) / 10
≈ 88.7 m.

Step 3: Calculate the range for Test III.

R = (36² × sin 50°) / 10
= (1296 × 0.766) / 10
≈ 99.3 m.

Step 4: Calculate the range for Test IV.

Since sin 120° = sin 60° = 0.866,

R = (24² × sin 120°) / 10
= (576 × 0.866) / 10
≈ 49.9 m.

Step 5: Compare the calculated ranges.

• Test I = 78.4 m
• Test II ≈ 88.7 m
• Test III ≈ 99.3 m
• Test IV ≈ 49.9 m

The greatest horizontal range is produced by Test III.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because although a launch angle of 45° gives the maximum range for a given launch speed, its launch speed is lower than that of Test III, resulting in a smaller range.
Option B is incorrect because its calculated range is approximately 88.7 m, which is less than the 99.3 m obtained for Test III.
Option D is incorrect because although 60° is complementary to 30°, the much lower launch speed of 24 m s⁻¹ produces the shortest range among the four tests.
explanation.exam_tip: In JAMB comparison questions, avoid selecting the projectile with the largest angle or the highest speed without calculation. Always evaluate both u² and sin 2θ using the range equation before making a conclusion.
explanation.simple_explanation: The horizontal range depends on two factors working together: the square of the launch speed and the value of sin 2θ. After calculating the range for each test, Test III gives the largest value of approximately 99.3 m. Therefore, it travels the greatest horizontal distance.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Range of a Projectile for a complete revision of the range equation, complementary angles, maximum range, comparison techniques and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional exam-focused practice.

question:
While reviewing the flight of a football during a training session, a coach asks the players to identify the meaning of the maximum height of a projectile.

Which of the following best describes the maximum height attained by the football?

options.A: The greatest vertical distance reached above its point of projection before descending
options.B: The total distance travelled by the football from launch to landing
options.C: The horizontal distance covered before the football reaches the ground
options.D: The point where both the horizontal and vertical components of velocity become zero

answer_text: The greatest vertical distance reached above its point of projection before descending

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the meaning of the maximum height of a projectile rather than your ability to perform calculations.
explanation.principle:
A projectile is any object launched into the air that subsequently moves under the influence of gravity alone, neglecting air resistance.

As the projectile rises, gravity continuously reduces its vertical component of velocity while its horizontal component remains unchanged.

The maximum height is the highest vertical position reached above the point of projection before the projectile begins to descend.

At this highest point:

• The vertical velocity is zero.
• The horizontal velocity is still present.
• The projectile continues moving forward even though it has stopped moving upward momentarily.
explanation.definitions:
Maximum height is the greatest vertical distance reached by a projectile above its point of projection.

Vertical velocity is the upward or downward component of the projectile's velocity.

Horizontal velocity is the component of velocity parallel to the ground and remains constant when air resistance is neglected.
explanation.foundational_knowledge: Many students wrongly believe that a projectile stops completely at its highest point. In reality, only the upward motion stops because the vertical component of velocity becomes zero. The projectile still possesses horizontal velocity and therefore continues moving forward.
steps: Step 1: Identify what is being defined. The question asks for the meaning of maximum height.
Step 2: Recall that maximum height refers to the highest vertical position reached above the launch point.
Step 3: Compare the options. Only Option A correctly defines maximum height.
Step 4: Therefore, Option A is the correct answer.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because it describes the total path travelled by the projectile, not its maximum height.
Option C is incorrect because it refers to the horizontal range of the projectile.
Option D is incorrect because only the vertical component of velocity becomes zero at the highest point. The horizontal component remains constant.
common_misconceptions: Believing that the projectile stops completely at its highest point.
Confusing maximum height with the horizontal range.
Mistaking the total distance travelled for the maximum height.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB asks about the highest point of a projectile, remember that only the vertical velocity becomes zero. The projectile continues moving horizontally until it lands.
explanation.final_answer: A. The greatest vertical distance reached above its point of projection before descending
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine kicking a football into the air. It keeps rising until it reaches its highest point. That highest point above where it was kicked is called the maximum height. The ball is still moving forward at that instant even though it has stopped moving upward.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Maximum Height to learn the meaning of maximum height, the behaviour of velocity components at the highest point, and worked JAMB-style examples. Continue your preparation with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre for additional concept reviews and exam-focused practice.

question:
A basketball coach reviews the trajectory of a player's shot and asks the team to identify the instant when the ball is at its maximum height.

Which of the following observations correctly identifies that point?

options.A: The ball has zero horizontal velocity but continues to accelerate vertically downward.
options.B: The ball has zero vertical velocity while its horizontal velocity remains unchanged.
options.C: Both the horizontal and vertical components of velocity become zero simultaneously.
options.D: The ball reaches the midpoint of its horizontal range.

answer_text: The ball has zero vertical velocity while its horizontal velocity remains unchanged.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests whether you can correctly identify the condition of a projectile at its maximum height.
explanation.principle:
A projectile moves under the influence of gravity after it is launched.

Its motion can be separated into two independent components:

• Horizontal motion, which has constant velocity when air resistance is neglected.
• Vertical motion, which is continuously affected by gravity.

As the projectile rises, gravity gradually reduces its vertical velocity until it becomes zero at the highest point. However, gravity does not affect the horizontal component of velocity, so the projectile continues moving forward.
explanation.definitions:
Maximum height is the highest vertical position reached by a projectile.

Vertical velocity is the upward or downward component of velocity.

Horizontal velocity is the component parallel to the ground and remains constant in ideal projectile motion.
explanation.foundational_knowledge: Many learners incorrectly think that the projectile stops completely at its highest point. In reality, only its upward motion stops momentarily. Its forward motion continues because the horizontal velocity is still present.
steps: Step 1: Recall the condition at maximum height.
Step 2: At this point, the vertical component of velocity becomes zero.
Step 3: The horizontal component remains constant because gravity acts only vertically.
Step 4: Therefore, the correct description is given in Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because the horizontal velocity does not become zero at maximum height.
Option C is incorrect because only the vertical component becomes zero. The projectile continues moving horizontally.

Option D is incorrect because the maximum height does not necessarily occur at the midpoint of the horizontal range in every projectile situation, especially when projection and landing heights differ.
common_misconceptions: Believing the projectile stops completely at the highest point.
Thinking gravity also removes the horizontal component of velocity.
Assuming maximum height is always located at the midpoint of the horizontal distance travelled.
explanation.exam_tip:
Whenever you encounter a JAMB question about the highest point of a projectile, immediately remember:

• Vertical velocity = 0.
• Horizontal velocity remains unchanged.

This simple fact answers many conceptual projectile-motion questions.
explanation.final_answer: B. The ball has zero vertical velocity while its horizontal velocity remains unchanged.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine throwing a basketball in an arc. At the highest point, the ball stops moving upward for an instant before falling back down. It is still moving forward toward the basket because its horizontal velocity has not changed.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Maximum Height to learn how velocity changes throughout projectile motion, identify the conditions at the highest point, and practise additional JAMB-style conceptual questions with the NaijaPrizeGate CBT Tutorial Centre.

question:
During a school Physics laboratory demonstration, a steel ball is projected from ground level with a speed of 26 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 40° to the horizontal. Neglect air resistance and take the acceleration due to gravity as 10 m s⁻².

What maximum height does the ball attain above the point of projection?

explanation.question_restate: This question requires you to calculate the greatest vertical height reached by a projectile using its launch speed and angle of projection.
explanation.principle:
When a projectile is launched at an angle to the horizontal, its initial velocity has two components:

• Horizontal component: u cos θ
• Vertical component: u sin θ

The maximum height depends only on the vertical component of the launch velocity because gravity acts vertically.

For a projectile that lands at the same level from which it was projected, the maximum height is given by:

H = (u² sin²θ)/(2g)

where:

• H = maximum height
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity.
explanation.definitions: Maximum height is the greatest vertical distance reached above the point of projection before the projectile begins to descend.
explanation.foundational_knowledge: Only the vertical component of the launch velocity determines how high a projectile rises. The horizontal component affects how far it travels, not its maximum height.
steps:
Step 1: Write the known values.

u = 26 m s⁻¹
θ = 40°
g = 10 m s⁻²

Step 2: Use the maximum-height formula.

H = (u² sin²θ)/(2g)

Step 3: Substitute the values.

H = (26² × sin²40°)/(2 × 10)

Using sin40° ≈ 0.643,

sin²40° ≈ 0.413.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

The calculated value corresponds to Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is approximately twice the correct value and may result from omitting the divisor 2 in the formula.
Option C is obtained by using an incorrect trigonometric value or formula.
Option D is too small and could result from using sin40° instead of sin²40°.
common_misconceptions: Using the range or time-of-flight formula instead of the maximum-height formula.
Using sinθ instead of sin²θ.
Forgetting to divide by 2g.
explanation.exam_tip: For maximum-height calculations, first identify the correct formula. Then evaluate sin²θ carefully before substituting into the equation. Many JAMB errors arise from using sinθ instead of sin²θ.
explanation.simple_explanation: The ball rises because of its upward velocity. Gravity gradually slows this upward motion until the ball reaches its highest point. Using the maximum-height formula gives approximately 13.95 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Maximum Height for detailed derivations, worked examples, calculator tips and additional JAMB-style questions on projectile height.

question:
An athlete launches a training javelin during a field practice with an initial speed of 38 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 55° to the horizontal. Neglect air resistance and take the acceleration due to gravity as 10 m s⁻².

What is the maximum height reached by the javelin above its point of projection?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your ability to calculate the maximum height attained by a projectile from its launch speed and angle of projection.
explanation.principle:
A projectile launched at an angle has both horizontal and vertical components of velocity. Only the vertical component determines how high the projectile rises because gravity acts vertically.

For projection and landing at the same level, the maximum height is given by:

H = (u² sin²θ)/(2g)

where:

• H = maximum height
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity.
explanation.definitions: Maximum height is the greatest vertical distance reached above the point of projection before the projectile begins to descend.
explanation.foundational_knowledge: The horizontal component affects the range of the projectile but does not influence its maximum height. The maximum height depends on the square of the vertical component of the launch velocity.
steps:
Step 1: Write the known values.

u = 38 m s⁻¹
θ = 55°
g = 10 m s⁻²

Step 2: Use the formula.

H = (u² sin²θ)/(2g)

Step 3: Substitute the values.

H = (38² × sin²55°)/20

Using sin55° ≈ 0.819,

sin²55° ≈ 0.671.

Step 5: Compare with the options.

The calculated value corresponds to Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is obtained by failing to divide by 2g.
Option C results from using an incorrect trigonometric value or computation.
Option D is approximately half the correct value and may result from an arithmetic error.
common_misconceptions: Using sinθ instead of sin²θ.
Using the range formula instead of the maximum-height formula.
Ignoring the factor 2g in the denominator.
explanation.exam_tip: For maximum-height problems, always square both the launch speed and the sine of the launch angle before dividing by 2g.
explanation.simple_explanation: The javelin rises until its vertical motion is completely reduced by gravity. Applying the maximum-height formula gives approximately 48.48 m.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Maximum Height for detailed derivations, worked examples and additional JAMB-style questions on projectile motion.

question:
Two javelins are projected from the same point at the same angle to the horizontal. The first leaves the thrower's hand with a speed of 20 m s⁻¹, while the second is launched at 34 m s⁻¹. Neglect air resistance.

If the angle of projection is unchanged for both throws, by what factor is the maximum height of the second javelin greater than that of the first?

explanation.question_restate: This question examines how changing the launch speed affects the maximum height of a projectile when the angle of projection remains constant.
explanation.principle:
The maximum height of a projectile projected and landing at the same level is given by:

H = (u²sin²θ)/(2g)

If the launch angle θ and gravitational acceleration g remain unchanged, then:

H ∝ u²

This means the maximum height is proportional to the square of the launch speed, not the launch speed itself.
explanation.definitions: Maximum height is the greatest vertical distance reached above the point of projection before the projectile starts descending.
explanation.foundational_knowledge: Since both projectiles are launched at the same angle, their values of sin²θ are identical. Therefore, only the square of their launch speeds determines the ratio of their maximum heights.
steps:
Step 1: Write the given speeds.

First javelin: u₁ = 20 m s⁻¹
Second javelin: u₂ = 34 m s⁻¹

Step 2: Apply the proportional relationship.

H₂/H₁ = (u₂/u₁)²

Step 3: Substitute the values.

H₂/H₁ = (34/20)²

Step 4: Evaluate the expression.

34/20 = 1.7

(1.7)² = 2.89

Step 5: Interpret the result.

The second javelin reaches a maximum height that is 2.89 times that of the first.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is obtained by comparing the launch speeds directly instead of squaring their ratio.
Option C results from multiplying the speeds incorrectly rather than comparing their squares.
Option D assumes that any increase in launch speed automatically quadruples the maximum height, which is not true in this case.
common_misconceptions: Assuming maximum height is directly proportional to launch speed.
Forgetting to square the ratio of the launch speeds.
Using the range formula instead of the maximum-height relationship.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever two projectiles are launched at the same angle under the same gravitational field, compare their maximum heights by squaring the ratio of their launch speeds.
explanation.simple_explanation:
The launch angle is the same for both javelins, so the only factor affecting the comparison is the square of the launch speed.

(34 ÷ 20)² = 2.89.

Hence, the second javelin rises 2.89 times higher than the first.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Maximum Height to learn how launch speed, launch angle and gravity influence the maximum height of projectiles, with worked JAMB-style examples and guided practice.

question:
During a fireworks display, two identical shells are launched from the same point with the same initial speed of 30 m s⁻¹. One is projected at an angle of 30° to the horizontal, while the other is projected at 70°. Neglect air resistance.

Which statement correctly compares their maximum heights?

options.A: The shell projected at 30° reaches the greater maximum height because it travels farther horizontally.
options.B: Both shells reach the same maximum height because they have the same launch speed.
options.C: The shell projected at 70° reaches the greater maximum height because its vertical component of velocity is larger.
options.D: The shell projected at 30° reaches the greater maximum height because it has the greater horizontal component of velocity.

answer_text: The shell projected at 70° reaches the greater maximum height because its vertical component of velocity is larger.

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of how the angle of projection affects the maximum height of a projectile when the launch speed remains constant.
explanation.principle:
For a projectile launched and landing at the same level,

H = (u²sin²θ)/(2g)

where:

H = maximum height
u = launch speed
θ = angle of projection
g = acceleration due to gravity

If the launch speed and gravitational acceleration remain constant, the maximum height depends only on sin²θ.
explanation.definitions: Maximum height is the greatest vertical distance reached by a projectile above its point of projection before it starts descending.
explanation.foundational_knowledge: The vertical component of the launch velocity is u sinθ. A larger launch angle produces a larger vertical component, allowing the projectile to rise higher. The horizontal component affects the range but does not determine the maximum height.
steps:
Step 1: Identify what is constant.

Both shells have the same launch speed and are subjected to the same gravitational acceleration.

Step 2: Compare the launch angles.

The first shell is projected at 30° while the second is projected at 70°.

Step 3: Compare their vertical velocity components.

Since sin70° > sin30°, the shell projected at 70° has the larger vertical component of velocity.

Step 4: Apply the maximum-height relationship.

Because H ∝ sin²θ when the launch speed is constant, the shell projected at 70° reaches a greater maximum height.

Step 5: Select the correct option.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option C.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because greater horizontal range does not imply greater maximum height.
Option B is incorrect because maximum height depends on both launch speed and launch angle.
Option D is incorrect because the horizontal component affects horizontal motion, not the maximum vertical height.
common_misconceptions: Believing that projectiles with the same launch speed always attain the same maximum height.
Assuming that a greater horizontal component produces a greater maximum height.
Confusing the factors affecting range with those affecting maximum height.
explanation.exam_tip: For questions comparing maximum heights at the same launch speed, focus on the vertical component of velocity. The larger the value of sinθ, the greater the maximum height.
explanation.final_answer: C. The shell projected at 70° reaches the greater maximum height because its vertical component of velocity is larger.
explanation.simple_explanation: Both shells start with the same speed, but the one launched at 70° directs more of that speed upward. This larger vertical component allows it to climb higher before gravity brings it back down.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Maximum Height to study how launch speed, launch angle and gravity affect the maximum height of projectiles, with worked JAMB-style examples and exam-focused practice.

question: A candidate performing a WAEC Physics practical projects a ball with an initial speed of 32 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 45° to the horizontal. Neglecting air resistance, what is the vertical velocity of the ball at its maximum height?

explanation.question_restate: This question tests your understanding of the vertical velocity of a projectile when it reaches its maximum height.
explanation.principle:
A projectile has two independent components of motion:

• Horizontal motion, which remains uniform because no horizontal force acts on the projectile.

• Vertical motion, which is uniformly accelerated downward by gravity.

At the maximum height, gravity has reduced the upward vertical component of velocity to zero before the projectile starts descending.

Therefore,

vᵧ = 0 m s⁻¹

This is true regardless of the launch speed or angle, provided air resistance is neglected.
explanation.definitions:
Vertical velocity is the component of a projectile's velocity acting in the upward or downward direction.

Maximum height is the highest point reached by the projectile during its motion.
explanation.foundational_knowledge: Many students mistakenly believe that the projectile stops completely at the highest point. In reality, only the vertical component of velocity becomes zero. The horizontal component remains unchanged, so the projectile continues moving forward.
steps:
Step 1: Identify the quantity required.

The question asks for the vertical velocity at the highest point of the motion.

Step 2: Recall the projectile principle.

At maximum height,

vᵧ = 0 m s⁻¹.

Step 3: Examine the given values.

Although the launch speed is 32 m s⁻¹ and the projection angle is 45°, these values do not affect the vertical velocity at the highest point because gravity always reduces the upward vertical component to zero before descent begins.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option A.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option B is incorrect because 16 m s⁻¹ is not the vertical velocity at maximum height.
Option C is incorrect because 22.6 m s⁻¹ is approximately the initial vertical component of velocity (32 sin45°), not the velocity at the highest point.
Option D is incorrect because 32 m s⁻¹ is the launch speed, not the vertical velocity at maximum height.
common_misconceptions: Believing that the projectile stops completely at maximum height.
Confusing the initial vertical component of velocity with the vertical velocity at maximum height.
Assuming the launch speed remains the vertical velocity throughout the motion.
explanation.exam_tip: Whenever JAMB asks about the velocity at the highest point of a projectile, first determine whether it refers to the vertical component or the total velocity. The vertical velocity is always zero at maximum height, but the projectile still has a horizontal velocity.
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine throwing a ball upward. As it rises, it slows down because of gravity. At the highest point, it stops moving upward for a brief instant before falling. At that instant, its vertical velocity is zero, even though it is still moving horizontally.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Maximum Height to learn why the vertical velocity becomes zero at the highest point while the horizontal velocity remains constant, with worked JAMB-style examples and exam-focused practice.

question: Scientists in a university Physics laboratory compare two projectiles launched simultaneously from the same level under identical gravitational conditions. Projectile P is projected with a speed of 24 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 35°, while Projectile Q is projected with a speed of 20 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 60°. Ignoring air resistance, which projectile reaches the greater maximum height?

options.A: Projectile P because it has the greater launch speed
options.B: Projectile Q because it has the larger vertical component of launch velocity
options.C: Both projectiles reach the same maximum height
options.D: The information provided is insufficient to determine

answer_text: Projectile Q because it has the larger vertical component of launch velocity

explanation.question_restate: This question compares the maximum heights reached by two projectiles launched with different speeds and projection angles.
explanation.principle:
The maximum height of a projectile depends on its initial vertical component of velocity.

For projection and landing at the same level,

H = (u²sin²θ)/(2g)

where:

• H = maximum height
• u = launch speed
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity

A larger launch speed alone does not necessarily produce a greater maximum height. The vertical component of velocity, u sinθ, is the determining factor.
explanation.definitions:
Maximum height is the greatest vertical distance reached by a projectile above its point of projection.

The vertical component of launch velocity is the upward part of the initial velocity given by u sinθ.
explanation.foundational_knowledge: Gravity acts only in the vertical direction. Therefore, the maximum height depends on how much of the launch speed is directed vertically, not on the total launch speed or the horizontal component.
steps:
Step 1: Determine the vertical component of each projectile.

Projectile P:

uᵧ = 24 × sin35° ≈ 24 × 0.574 ≈ 13.8 m s⁻¹.

Projectile Q:

uᵧ = 20 × sin60° ≈ 20 × 0.866 ≈ 17.3 m s⁻¹.

Step 2: Compare the vertical components.

Projectile Q has the larger vertical component of launch velocity.

Step 3: Apply the maximum-height principle.

Since maximum height is proportional to the square of the vertical component of velocity,

H ∝ (u sinθ)².

Therefore, Projectile Q reaches the greater maximum height.

Step 4: Select the correct option.

The correct answer is Option B.
why_other_options_are_wrong: Option A is incorrect because maximum height does not depend solely on the total launch speed.
Option C is incorrect because the projectiles have different vertical components of launch velocity, so their maximum heights are different.
Option D is incorrect because sufficient information has been provided to compare the maximum heights.
common_misconceptions: Assuming the projectile with the greater launch speed always reaches the greater maximum height.
Ignoring the effect of the launch angle on the vertical component of velocity.
Confusing maximum height with horizontal range.
explanation.exam_tip: When comparing maximum heights, first compare the values of u sinθ or u²sin²θ. Do not base your answer on launch speed alone.
explanation.final_answer: B. Projectile Q because it has the larger vertical component of launch velocity
explanation.simple_explanation: Imagine two people throwing balls. One throws faster but almost forward, while the other throws slightly slower but much more upward. The second ball rises higher because more of its speed is directed vertically.
explanation.tutorial_reference: Visit Tutorials → Physics → Projectile and Relative Motion → Maximum Height to learn how launch speed and launch angle combine to determine the maximum height of a projectile, with worked JAMB-style comparison problems and exam-focused practice.

question: Engineers testing a launch mechanism observe that a projectile reaches a maximum height of 36.45 m when projected at an angle of 60° above the horizontal. Assuming the projectile lands at the same level from which it was launched and taking g = 10 m s⁻², what was the initial speed of projection?

explanation.question_restate: This is a reverse projectile problem in which the maximum height and angle of projection are known, while the initial launch speed is to be determined.
explanation.principle:
For a projectile that lands at the same level from which it is projected, the maximum height is given by:

H = (u²sin²θ)/(2g)

where:

• H = maximum height
• u = initial speed of projection
• θ = angle of projection
• g = acceleration due to gravity

In reverse problems, the formula is rearranged to determine the unknown launch speed.
explanation.definitions:
Maximum height is the highest vertical position reached by a projectile before it begins to descend.

The launch speed is the magnitude of the initial velocity at the instant of projection.
explanation.foundational_knowledge: Only the vertical component of the launch velocity contributes to the maximum height. Since u sinθ determines the upward motion, the launch speed can be calculated once the maximum height and angle are known.
explanation.formula_introduction:
Rearranging the maximum-height equation:

u² = (2gH)/(sin²θ)

Therefore,

u = √[(2gH)/(sin²θ)]
steps:
Step 1: Write the known values.

H = 36.45 m
θ = 60°
g = 10 m s⁻²

Step 2: Evaluate sin²60°.

sin60° = √3/2 ≈ 0.866

sin²60° = 0.75

Step 3: Substitute into the rearranged formula.

u² = (2 × 10 × 36.45)/0.75

= 729/0.75

= 972

Step 4: Determine the launch speed.

u = √972

≈ 31.2 m s⁻¹

The closest option is 30 m s⁻¹.

Step 5: Select the correct answer.
